content.full_text:
Re
Fern PoLrrrcAL PnacrrcEs CouMrssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
Ianuary 29,2003
Gregory D. Totten, District Attorney
Office of the District Attorney
County of Ventura
Hall ofJustice
800 South Victoria Avenue
Ventura, CA 93009
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-03-004
Dear Mr. Totten:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
QUESTION
You plan to use alarge meeting hall at California State University, Channel
Islands, for an election victory celebration. If the university waived the set fee of $1,750
that the university usually charges for use of the meeting hall, how would this transaction
be reported?
CONCLUSION
If the university waived the fee for your use of the hall, ttb transaction would be
reportable as a $1,750 non-monetary contribution to your campaign comntittee from the
university. However, university policy and laws outside the Act concerning the use of
public funds, may prohibit the university from making a political contribution. If your
committee pays the usual fee for the use of the hall, no contribution will result.
FACTS
You plan on hosting alarge party to celebrate your recent election to the office of
Ventura County District Attorney. You plan to pay the expenses of the party from
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appaar at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
r-n


File No. A-03-004
Page No. 2
campaign funds, and send party invitations directly to known supporters and
acquaintances.
In anticipation of a large number of attendees (500-700), you would like to use a
meeting hall that is available for large functions on the campus of the California State
University, Channel Islands. The university has a set fee of $1,750, which it charges for
the use of the facility. You ask how the transaction would be reported if the university
waived the facility use fee.
ANALYSIS
The Act specifically provides that campugn funds may be used for an election
victory celebration in section 89513(DQ). The Act's use of campaign funds provisions
(sections 89510-89522) are designed to ensure that campaign funds are used for
electioneering and officeholder pulposes, and are not expended for a candidate or
officeholder's personal putposes. Accordingly, the Act provides that all contributions
deposited into a campaign account are "held in trust for expenses associated with the
election of the candidate or for expenses associated with holding office." (Section
89s10.)
Generally, an expenditure related to seeking or holding office is permissible under
the Act if it is reasonably related to a political, legislative or governmental putpose.
(Section 89512.) Certain specified uses of campaign funds, including those that are
considered to confer a "substantial personal benefit"2 on the candidate must meet a higher
standard and be directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
(Section 89512.) With respect to an election victory celebration, section 89513(0(2)
states:
"An election victory celebration or similar campaign event . . . are considered to
be directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose... ."
(Emphasis added.)
To determine whether the university's waiver of the facility fee would constitute a
contribution under the Act, we turn to the definition of "contribution" in section 82015 of
the Act, which states:
"(a) 'Contribution' means apayment, a forgiveness of a loart, apayment of a
loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the
extent that fulI and adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear from the
surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes."
fl...fl
2 "substantial personal benefit" is defined to mean "an expenditure of campaign funds which
results in a direct personal benefit with a value of more than two hundred dollars ($200) to a candidate,
elected officer, or any individual or individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign
funds held by a committee." ($ 8951l(bx3).)


File No. ,{-03-004
Page No. 3
"(c) The term 'contribution' includes the purchase of tickets for events such as
dinners, luncheons, rallies, and similar fundraising events; the candidate,s own
money or property used on behalf of his or her.candidacy ...; the granting of
discounts or rebates not extended to the public generally or thegranting of
discounts or rebates by televisions and radio stations and newspapers not
extended on an equal basis to all candidates for the same office; the payment of
compensation by any person for the personal services or expenses of any other
person if the services are rendered or expenses incurred on behalf of a candidate
or committee without payment of full and adequate consideration....,, (Emphasis
added.)
Here, under section 89513(0(2), the election victory celebration is considered to
be directly related to a political purpose and thus, if the university donated the use of the
hall, it would be for a political pu{pose. In addition, waiver of the facility fee by the
trniversity would constitute the granting of a discount or rebate to your campaign that is
not extended to the public generally under section 82015(c). Thus, if the university
waived the facility fee, it would constitute a non-monetary contribution by the university
to your campaign committee in the amount of $1,750. Such a conkibution would be
reportable under regulation 18420, copy enclosed. AltemativelS if your campaign
committee pays the $1,750 fee for the use of the hall, no contribution will r"s,rlt.
As a public institution, university policies and laws outside the Act, may prohibit
the university from using public funds for apolitical conkibution. (See comment to
regulation 18420, enclosed, and Government Code $ 8314.) You or university staff may
want to contact the Attorney General's office concerning laws outside the Act preventing
the use of public funds for political purposes.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
t/" P h)nr,.,-,/
By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
HPwjg
